Four years in, here's my take on Pope Francis. On the negative side, he's an undisciplined communicator. He tends to ramble when he speaks off the cuff and, now and then, he says things that he (or his aides) have to walk back, creating too many unfortunate distractions. He can be ambiguous when speaking about Church teaching, tending more toward platitudes than clarity, and he's insensitive and awkward when speaking about women. 

On the plus side, he understands and articulates what's essential and urgent about the Church (mercy, solidarity with the poor and suffering, acting as Christ in the world) and what's excess baggage that should be jettisoned (triumphalism, cancerous clericalism, rigid legalism over compassion.)

Although you are right that recent biology provides a sound basis for rethinking the notion that fully human life begins with conception, that is not the problem with the rabid anti-abortion position of so many American Catholics. The precautionary principle is sound moral reasoning. The moral dilemma is otherwise.

Catholic morality is equally wedding to (1) a rejection of abortion and (2) freedom of conscience. In pluralist societies where huge numbers of rational, sober, fellow citizens, with different foundations than ours for their moral philosophy, mange to believe that abortion, at least in the earlier stages of pregnancy, is not the taking of human life, we have an absolute obligation to respect their conscience. Therefore, the law which governs all citizen must allow abortion.  We are obliged to refrain, but our conviction that early human life is sacred, that it is from some not fully clear early time "immortal,"  cannot, by our own principles, be imposed on others.

God knows, our Church have changed its minds on many things over the centuries, and that de facto development of doctrine is critically important in our age, given the nature of traditionalist/progressive battles.

But I suggest, Elagablus, that the evolution of Christian thought on abortion is not a helpful example. The very real changes over time are due to evolving understandings of the biology of procreation. For instance, Acquinas based his views on newly rediscovered Aristotelian biology (On the Generation of Animals) and his metaphysics. 

In this particular case, I believe the thrust of the Catholic position has been consistent--life in the womb, whenever and however that was understood to begin at different times in our history, is sacred, and not to be violated--regardless of lack of scriptural warrant.

The reason for supporting legalized abortion, IMHO, is not because we are uncertain about the fetus, but because we have no right to impose our morality on others.

A couple of scary lines:  Catholic agencies are to "strengthen their core services on the path to deepening the understanding of what it means to be Catholic."  And later:  "...strengthen Catholic identity among the laypeople."  Bishops have a very narrow view of what Catholic identity means - subservience, obedience to rules and the "tradition" which no longer works in the modern world.  This is pulling into a ghetto mentality, refusing to work with other organizations on a common issue if that other organization also supports something they consider non-Catholic, like gay marriage or condoms to prevent the spread of AIDS.  

Trying to recreate power structures a la 1500?

Speak for yourself, sir.  Just because a bunch of liberal dissenting "Catholics" would not miss the bishops until Confirmation does not entail everyone would. 

The gold standard for what is or is not important is not, and never has been liberal dissenting "Catholics."

I concede that the bishops  have been addressing topics previously ignored, or very briefly noted.  Perhaps they are becoming aware of the bitterness everywhere within our political discourse, and feel as if they need to say something.  The problem is that far too many people have marginalized them, on both sides.  Some only wish to hear about abortion and gay marriage and view all other issues to be ones in which there is, or should be, no official Church position.  Those on the other side often do not see newer, more nuanced statements because they have dismissed the bishops already as Republican scolds.  Either way, if our bishops are so inclined, they could become a force for good, if they can encourage at least Catholics to be more open-minded with each other. God knows, we need to do something before we destroy the nation we all purport to love.
